title: It Is, Moreover, Proved by Moses in the Beginning of the Holy Scriptures.

source.title: ANF05. Fathers of the Third Century: Hippolytus, Cyprian, Caius, Novatian, Appendix

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Chapter XVII.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Argument.—It Is, Moreover, Proved by Moses in the Beginning of the Holy Scriptures.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: What if Moses pursues this same rule of truth, and delivers to us in the beginning of his sacred writings, this principle by which we may learn that all things were created and founded by the Son of God, that is, by the Word of God?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: For He says the same that John and the rest say; nay, both John and the others are perceived to have received from Him what they say.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: For if John says, “All things were made by Him, and without Him was nothing made,” the prophet David too says, “I tell my works to the King.”

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Moses, moreover, introduces God commanding that there should be light at the first, that the heaven should be established, that the waters should be gathered into one place, that the dry land should appear, that the fruit should be brought forth according to its seed, that the animals should be produced, that lights should be established in heaven, and stars.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: He shows that none other was then present to God—by whom these works were commanded that they should be made—than He by whom all things were made, and without whom nothing was made.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: And if He is the Word of God—“for my heart has uttered forth a good Word”—He shows that in the beginning the Word was, and that this Word was with the Father, and besides that the Word was God, and that all things were made by Him.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Moreover, this “Word was made flesh and dwelt among us,”—to wit, Christ the Son of God; whom both on receiving subsequently as man according to the flesh, and seeing before the foundation of the world to be the Word of God, and God, we reasonably, according to the instruction of the Old and New Testament, believe and hold to be as well God as man, Christ Jesus.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: What if the same Moses introduces God saying, “Let us make man after our image and likeness;” and below, “And God made man; in the image of God made He him, male and female made He them?”

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: If, as we have already shown, it is the Son of God by whom all things were made, certainly it was the Son of God by whom also man was ordained, on whose account all things were made.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Moreover, when God commands that man should be made, He is said to be God who makes man; but the Son of God makes man, that is to say, the Word of God, “by whom all things were made, and without whom nothing was made.”

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: And this Word was made flesh, and dwelt among us: therefore Christ is God; therefore man was made by Christ as by the Son of God.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: But God made man in the image of God; He is therefore God who made man in the image of God; therefore Christ is God: so that with reason neither does the testimony of the Old Testament waver concerning the person of Christ, being supported by the manifestation of the New Testament; nor is the power of the New Testament detracted from, while its truth is resting on the roots of the same Old Testament.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Whence they who presume Christ the Son of God and man to be only man, and not God also, do so in opposition to both Old and New Testaments, in that they corrupt the authority and the truth both of the Old and New Testaments.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: What if the same Moses everywhere introduces God the Father infinite and without end, not as being enclosed in any place, but as one who includes every place; nor as one who is in a place, but rather one in whom every place is, containing all things and embracing all things, so that with reason He can neither descend nor ascend, because He Himself both contains and fills all things, and yet nevertheless introduces God descending to consider the tower which the sons of men were building, asking and saying, “Come;” and then, “Let us go down and there confound their tongues, that each one may not understand the words of his neighbour.”

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Whom do they pretend here to have been the God who descended to that tower, and asking to visit those men at that time?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: God the Father?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Then thus He is enclosed in a place; and how does He embrace all things?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Or does He say that it is an angel descending with angels, and saying, “Come;” and subsequently, “Let us go down and there confound their tongues?”

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: And yet in Deuteronomy we observe that God told these things, and that God said, where it is written, “When He scattered abroad the children of Adam, He determined the bounds of the nations according to the number of the angels of God.”

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Neither, therefore, did the Father descend, as the subject itself indicates; nor did an angel command these things, as the fact shows.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Then it remains that He must have descended, of whom the Apostle Paul says, “He who descended is the same who ascended above all the heavens, that He might fill all things,” that is, the Son of God, the Word of God.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: But the Word of God was made flesh, and dwelt among us.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: This must be Christ.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Therefore Christ must be declared to be God.
